Overcoming Digital Twin Implementation Challenges
=================================================

Author: [Ankitha VP](https://www.toobler.com/blog/authors/ankitha-vp)

February 28, 2025

![Image 9: Overcoming Digital Twin Implementation Challenges](https://img2.storyblok.com/filters:format(webp)/f/122804/1600x597/e0b343871e/toobler-overcoming-challenges-in-digital-twin-implementation-strategies-and-solutions-banner.webp)

 Understanding the Landscape of Digital Twin Implementation

 Identifying Common Challenges in Digital Twin Implementation

 Strategies and Solutions for Overcoming Implementation Hurdles

 Best Practices for Digital Twin Implementation Across Various Industries

 Developing a Roadmap for Digital Twin Integration: A step by step guide

 Conclusion

 FAQs

Imagine having a real-time [digital twin model](https://www.toobler.com/blog/digital-twin-model)of your entire business that boosts efficiency, predicts issues, and drives growth. Sounds game-changing, right?

Yet, many companies struggle to implement [digital twin technology](https://www.toobler.com/digital-twin-solutions) due to unexpected challenges.

Despite their benefits, digital twins come with integration hurdles, high costs, and security risks. A [survey](https://www.mdpi.com/2076-3417/14/23/10933)found that these challenges make it hard for businesses to scale their[digital twin projects](https://www.toobler.com/blog/digital-twin-in-manufacturing).

So, what’s stopping companies from unlocking their full potential? This blog will break down the everyday challenges of digital twins, explore their key drawbacks, and provide practical solutions to help businesses implement [digital twins](https://www.toobler.com/blog/what-is-digital-twin) successfully.

Let’s dive in.

Understanding the Landscape of Digital Twin Implementation
----------------------------------------------------------

Diving into the world of digital twins is like stepping into a new dimension where every aspect of your physical business has a digital shadow.

Right now, [digital twin technology](https://www.toobler.com/blog/what-is-digital-twin) is like a rising star in the tech world – it's getting a lot of attention, and for good reason. Businesses across various sectors are starting to see just how much value these virtual models can add. From manufacturing floors to city infrastructures, [digital twins' use cases](https://www.toobler.com/blog/digital-twin-use-cases) are endless as they are helping companies predict problems before they happen, save money, and make smarter decisions.

Digital Twin technology is evolving rapidly, becoming more sophisticated and accessible. Thus, it is essential to know about the benefits and challenges of digital twin technology.

Before we can reap the benefits, we must grasp the terrain – the tech requirements, the data streams, and the integration points.

Suggested read: [What are the benefits of digital twin?](https://www.toobler.com/blog/benefits-of-digital-twins)

Identifying Common Challenges in Digital Twin Implementation
------------------------------------------------------------

![Image 10](https://a.storyblok.com/f/122804/1600x800/2e7f7e1776/identifying-common-challenges-in-digital-twin-implementation.webp)

When it comes to rolling out digital twins, it's not all about the high-tech glitz and glam. Organizations often bump into a few common problems with digital twins.

This section will showcase how to implement digital twins by being aware of the most common digital twins disadvantages or problems with their widespread use.

### 1. Data Complexity and Quality

Data is the foundation of a digital twin. However, the data must be precise, timely, and in an accessible format—not just any data will do. It can be difficult to manage data complexity and ensure quality, particularly when combining data from several sources.

### 2. System Integration

Digital twins are not isolated entities. They must integrate with current technology and systems. Because it frequently entails integrating new software with historical systems that weren't intended to communicate with one another, this integration can be challenging.

### 3. Technical Expertise

The usage of digital twins has a severe learning curve. It is quite difficult to find or train people who are familiar with the complexities involved in creating and operating these systems.

### 4. Cost and ROI Concerns

Training and new technology investments are necessary for the implementation of digital twins. Businesses frequently struggle to secure budget approval, estimate the return on investment, and justify the expense. This is one of the major digital twin disadvantages while implementing its across industries.

### 5. Scalability

While it's easy to start small, it might be challenging to scale[digital twin software](https://www.toobler.com/blog/types-of-digital-twin-software)throughout an organization or for several products. Scaling up presents challenges for resource management and consistency.

### 6. Cybersecurity

Risk increases in tandem with a stronger connection. Businesses have a crucial problem in ensuring the security of digital twins and the preservation of confidential information.

### 7. Cultural Resistance

We don't always embrace change. Internally, among those used to more conventional approaches, there may be opposition to the introduction of new technologies such as digital twins.

### 8. Regulatory Compliance

The implementation of digital twins may encounter legislative obstacles specific to the industry, particularly with regard to use of data and confidentiality.

### 9. Real-time Data Processing

Real-time data is essential for digital twins, but processing it fast and effectively requires a strong IT infrastructure, which presents a challenge for some businesses.

### 10. Long-term Maintenance

For digital twins to continue being accurate and helpful, constant upkeep is necessary. This kind of long-term dedication can be difficult, particularly when company requirements and technology change.

Companies can unleash new levels of productivity and creativity by successfully implementing a digital twin by tackling these obstacles head-on.

In the next section, let's examine their answers.

Strategies and Solutions for Overcoming Implementation Hurdles
--------------------------------------------------------------

![Image 11: Strategies and Solutions for Overcoming Implementation Hurdles](https://a.storyblok.com/f/122804/1600x800/064eefc1d4/strategies-and-solutions-for-overcoming-implementation-hurdles.webp)

Implementing digital twins brings unique challenges. Yet strategic [digital twin solutions](https://www.toobler.com/blog/digital-twin-solutions-manufacturing)exist to turn these hurdles into triumphs. Here, we'll explore effective tactics to overcome common problems with digital twins, setting your digital twin project on a course for success.

### 1. Data Complexity and Quality

Implement robust data governance frameworks to ensure data accuracy and consistency. Use advanced analytics and AI to cleanse and structure data, making it more manageable.

### 2. System Integration

Adopt middleware solutions that can act as a bridge between new digital twin technologies and legacy systems. Use APIs and microservices architecture to facilitate smoother integration.

### 3. Technical Expertise

Invest in training programs to upskill existing staff. Alternatively, consider hiring specialists or partnering with technology providers who have expertise in digital twin technology.

### 4. Cost and ROI Concerns

Start with a pilot project to demonstrate the value of digital twins, which can help in securing buy-in for further investment. Use predictive analytics to more accurately forecast ROI.

### 5. Scalability

Develop a modular approach to digital twin implementation, allowing for small-scale successes to be expanded systematically. Ensure your [digital twin platform](https://www.toobler.com/blog/digital-twin-platforms-in-industries) is designed with scalability in mind from the outset.

### 6. Cybersecurity

Implement state-of-the-art cybersecurity measures, including encryption and regular security audits. Also, ensure compliance with data protection regulations to safeguard information.

### 7. Cultural Resistance

Engage in change management practices. To promote a culture that welcomes digital innovation, make sure the advantages are well communicated and include important stakeholders early in the process.

### 8. Regulatory Compliance

To guarantee compliance, stay up to date on pertinent rules and consult with legal professionals. Take regulatory standards into account when creating your digital twin's design.

### 9. Real-time Data Processing

Invest in high-performance computing resources and edge computing capabilities to process data in real-time. This ensures your digital twin can operate with minimal latency.

### 10. Long-term Maintenance

For your digital twin, create a clear servicing schedule that includes regular improvements and modifications. With your technology provider, think about a subscription plan to guarantee ongoing upkeep and assistance.

Best Practices for Digital Twin Implementation Across Various Industries
------------------------------------------------------------------------

You may wonder, what digital twin challenges do you solve?

[Digital twin software for business](https://www.toobler.com/blog/digital-twin-software-different-industries)transcends industry boundaries, each with its best practices for successful implementation. In the following section, we'll delve into these industry-specific strategies, distilling the wisdom that leads to digital twin excellence across the board.

### Manufacturing

To show how [digital twins in manufacturing](https://www.toobler.com/blog/digital-twins-in-manufacturing) can improve efficiency and decrease downtime, begin with a pilot project that focuses on a crucial portion of the manufacturing line.

Make sure the digital twin has an effortless connection for real-time monitoring and predictive maintenance with the current IoT devices and industrial execution systems (MES).

**Please read:**[How digital twin projects are transforming the Manufacturing industry](https://www.toobler.com/blog/digital-twin-in-manufacturing)

### Healthcare

[In healthcare](https://www.toobler.com/blog/digital-twin-in-healthcare), patient privacy and data security are paramount. Implement digital twins with a strong emphasis on compliance with health data standards such as HIPAA.

[Digital twins in clinical trials](https://www.toobler.com/blog/digital-twins-in-clinical-trials) help to simulate patient flows and improve care delivery without compromising patient confidentiality. Engage clinical staff early to ensure the digital twin is aligned with patient care objectives.

**Also read:**[Implementing Digital twins in Healthcare: Challenges & Solutions](https://www.toobler.com/blog/overcome-challenges-in-implementing-digital-twins-in-healthcare)

### Automotive

In order to expedite research and development and enhance safety, the[digital twin in the automotive industry](https://www.toobler.com/blog/digital-twin-automotive-industry) should concentrate on its capacity to replicate and evaluate car components under diverse circumstances. By predicting maintenance requirements and optimizing inventories, integration with supply chain management systems can lower costs and improve production cycles.

**Suggested read:**[How Digital Twin Can Train Workers in the Automotive Industry](https://www.toobler.com/blog/digital-twin-to-train-workers-in-automotive-industry)

### Urban Planning and Smart Cities

In order to replicate entire cities,[digital twins for urban planning](https://www.toobler.com/blog/digital-twins-in-smart-cities) should be scalable and capable of combining data from a variety of sources, such as utility usage, population growth, and traffic patterns. Working together with engineers, public servants, and city planners is essential to developing a dynamic model that guides emergency response plans and sustainable growth.

### Oil and Gas

Because they can improve operating safety and identify maintenance needs, [digital twins in the oil and gas sector](https://www.toobler.com/blog/digital-twin-oil-and-gas)[](https://www.toobler.com/blog/digital-twin-oil-and-gas)are a valuable tool. In order to prevent machinery breakdowns and improve maintenance schedules, reducing delay and guaranteeing employee security, it is imperative to incorporate real-time data from drilling equipment and sensors.

Organisations may guarantee the technical soundness and compliance of their [digital twin implementations](https://www.toobler.com/blog/digital-twin-implementation) with industry-specific best practises, while also meeting the specific requirements and regulatory frameworks of their respective domains.

Developing a Roadmap for Digital Twin Integration: A step by step guide
-----------------------------------------------------------------------

![Image 12: A roadmap for Digital Twin integration](https://a.storyblok.com/f/122804/1600x1200/ec273d3a32/a-roadmap-for-digital-twin-integration.webp)

A well-organized plan is necessary to realize every advantage of digital twins. This strategy serves as a roadmap, assisting enterprises in the difficult task of combining digital twin technology with their current processes and systems, guaranteeing a seamless transition and a direct route to accomplishing their strategic goals.

### STEP 1: Set Clear Objectives

Describe the goals of your digital twin, concentrating on particular company outcomes.

### STEP 2: Evaluate Infrastructure

Assess your current systems and technology to determine what can be utilized or needs upgrading for digital twin integration.

### STEP 3: Identify and Secure Data Sources

Determine the necessary data sources and establish protocols to ensure their security and integrity.

### STEP 4: Design and Test Prototype

Create a scalable digital twin architecture and develop a prototype to test functionality and performance.

### STEP 5: Implement Security Measures

Integrate cybersecurity and compliance measures to protect the digital twin and its data.

### STEP 6: Deploy and Train

Roll out the digital twin into your operations and provide comprehensive training to all relevant staff.

### STEP 7: Monitor and Optimize

Continuously review the digital twin's performance for improvements and scale up the solution as needed.

Conclusion
----------

With their unprecedented ability to provide a virtual window into an organization's internal workings, digital twins are revolutionizing the corporate world. We've gone over every detail of putting this technology into practice. FAQs
----

1.   **What are the challenges of digital twins?**

High requirements for data are one of the difficulties facing digital twins since precise models demand a lot of real-time data from IoT devices. Another big worry is cybersecurity, since digital twins are susceptible to hacking and data breaches. Attempting to align digital twins with current systems can also lead to integration difficulty.

Furthermore, the implementation and upkeep of digital twin technologies can be expensive and necessitate a large initial investment. Last but not least, expertise is crucial because the successful development and maintenance of digital twins need qualified experts.

**2. What are the risks of digital twins?**

Risks associated with digital twins include data security flaws that could expose private information to hackers. Additionally, there's a chance that old or erroneous data can result in poor conclusions or choices. When digital twins are combined with traditional systems, integration problems could occur, disrupting operations.

Financial risks are posed by high growth and maintenance costs, particularly for small enterprises. Furthermore, depending too much on digital twins might lead to dependency, which could affect vital operations in the unfortunate scenario when the technology malfunctions.

**3. Is it costly to implement a digital twin?**

Yes, because a digital twin requires sophisticated IoT sensors, data storage, and processing infrastructure, its implementation can be expensive. Software creation, integration with current systems, and continuing maintenance are additional expenses.

Additionally, setup and management require skilled workers, which raises costs. The investment can be significant, especially for smaller companies or organizations without an established digital infrastructure, even if costs may differ based on complexities and scope.

Highlights
----------

*   •Digital Twins have moved from idea to reality much faster in recent years. 
*   •The technology is poised to deliver upon its many promises in many industries. 
*   •The benefits of creating a Digital Twin solution are too vast and still not fully explored. 
*   •Digital Twins will combine with more technologies, such as augmented reality (AR) and AI for better connections, insights, and analytics. 

Abstract
--------

Applications of [Digital Twin technology](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/digital-twin-technology "Learn more about Digital Twin technology from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") have been growing at an exponential rate, and it is transforming the way businesses operate. In the past few years, [Digital Twins](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/digital-twin "Learn more about Digital Twins from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") leveraged vital [business applications](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/business-application "Learn more about business applications from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and it is predicted that the technology will expand to more applications, use cases, and industries. The purpose of this paper is to do a literature review and explore how Digital Twins streamline intelligent automation in different industries. This paper defines the concept, highlights the evolution and development of Digital Twins, reviews its key enabling technologies, examines its trends and challenges, and explores its applications in different industries.

1. Introduction
---------------

[Digital Twin](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/digital-twin "Learn more about Digital Twin from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is attracting attention from practitioners and scholars alike. Today, the technology is used across many industries to provide accurate virtual representations of objects and simulations of operational processes. In 2019, a Gartner survey revealed that Digital Twins were entering mainstream use by organizations. It predicted that 75 percent of [Internet of Things](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/internet-of-things "Learn more about Internet of Things from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (IoT) organizations also use [Digital Twin technology](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/digital-twin-technology "Learn more about Digital Twin technology from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") or plan to use it by 2020 [[1]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b1), [[2]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b2). Gartner also estimates that by 2027, over 40 percent of large companies worldwide will be using Digital Twin in their projects to increase revenue [[3]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b3).

Moreover, Global Market Insight estimated that the Digital Twin market size estimated in 2022 at $8 billion is expected to grow at around 25 percent [Compound Annual Growth Rate](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/compound-annual-growth-rate "Learn more about Compound Annual Growth Rate from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (CAGR) from 2023 to 2032 [[4]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b4). Finally, according to another recent report by global technology research, the Digital Twin market is set to grow by nearly $32 billion from 2021 to 2026 [[5]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b5). In addition, according to a 2022 report, nearly 60 percent of executives across a broad spectrum of industry plan to incorporate Digital Twins within their operations by 2028 [[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b6).

Digital Twin is a cutting-edge technology that has revolutionized the industry by mirroring almost every facet of a product, process, or service. It has the potential to replicate everything in the physical world in the digital space and provide engineers with feedback from the virtual world [[7]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b7). As a result, the technology enables companies to quickly detect and solve physical problems, design and build better products, and realize value and benefits faster than previously possible. Furthermore, the Digital Twin technology enables businesses to improve business processes and performance [[8]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b8).

The research started by developing key concepts based on a narrative literature review in the Scopus database and Google Scholar and searched for papers that included Digital Twin or Digital Twins in the title. Articles were selected from Journal articles, research articles, conference publications, research reports, or scientific encyclopedias. We reviewed the documents to find answers to the following questions: What is a Digital Twin, what technologies are used, what are the current applications of Digital Twins in different industries, and what are the challenges and opportunities facing this growing technology? We selected research papers that defined the concept, reviewed its related technologies, and highlighted its applications and trends in different industries. Section[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#sec2) provides the main perspectives and definitions of Digital Twins in literature. Section[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#sec3) reviews four enabling technologies of the Digital Twins. Section[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#sec4) studies Digital Twin applications and use cases of different industries. Section[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#sec5) highlights the challenges and opportunities of this technology. Finally, Section[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#sec6) provides a summary and conclusions. Section 7 provides references.

2. Background and definition
----------------------------

In 2003 Professor Grieves of the University of Michigan introduced the concept of [Digital Twins](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/digital-twin "Learn more about Digital Twins from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in a total [product lifecycle management](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/product-lifecycle-management "Learn more about product lifecycle management from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") course. It is also known as a digital mirror and digital mapping. Since then, its definition has continued to evolve as several scholars have provided varied definitions of this technology [[9]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b9), [[10]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b10), [[11]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b11), [[12]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b12), [[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b13), [[14]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b14). Encyclopedia of Production Engineering states that “_The Digital Twin is a representation of an active unique “product” which can be a real device, object, machine, service, intangible asset, or a system consisting of a product and its related services”_[[12]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b12)_._ In general, the Digital Twin is defined as virtual representations of [physical objects](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/physical-object "Learn more about physical objects from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") across the lifecycle that can be understood, learned, and reasoned with real-time data or a simulation model that acquires data from the field and triggers the operation of physical devices [[15]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b15), [[16]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b16).

Furthermore, Digital Twin was defined as the convergence between physical and virtual products [[17]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b17), [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b18). Fu et al. [[7]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b7) thought of the Digital Twin as a real-time digital representation of a physical object. They are remotely connected to real objects and provide rich representations of these objects. They go beyond static product designs, like CAD models, but comprise dynamic behavior [[19]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b19), [[20]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b20). A virtual replica of a real-world asset is obtained through constant data transmission allowing the digital version of the object to exist simultaneously with the physical one [[17]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b17). Digital Twin uses [big data](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/big-data "Learn more about big data from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") technology to mine hidden and effective data and to improve the intelligence and applicability of [Digital Twin technology](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/digital-twin-technology "Learn more about Digital Twin technology from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), especially for quick identification and evaluation of design flaws [[21]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b21).

Finally, Kritzinger et al. [[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b13) defined Digital Twin, within the field of manufacturing, based on the data integration level, which can be achieved between the physical product and its virtual representation. He identified three levels of integration, The Digital Model, Digital Shadow and the Digital Twin ([Fig.1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#fig1)) [[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b13), [[22]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b22).

A historic early application of Digital Twin technology is when NASA engineers used a simulator, a twin of the command module, and a separate twin of the module’s [electrical system](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/electrical-system "Learn more about electrical system from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to remedy and save Apollo 13 in 1970. NASA engineers completed the process in under two hours and saved the lives of the three astronauts on board. This was an extraordinary early application of this technology, and the technology has only matured since then [[23]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b23). Today, NASA uses Digital Twins to develop next-generation vehicles and aircraft.

The concept of Digital Twins is not new. However, Digital Twins have moved from idea to reality much faster in recent years. It is predicted that Digital Twins will be combined with more technologies such as speech capabilities, [augmented reality](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/augmented-reality "Learn more about augmented reality from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [IoT](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/internet-of-things "Learn more about IoT from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and [artificial intelligence](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/artificial-intelligence "Learn more about artificial intelligence from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (AI). As a result, Gartner included Digital Twins on its list of top 10 technology trends for 2017 [[24]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b24). Gartner also predicted that half of the large industrial firms to use Digital Twins in crucial [business applications](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/business-application "Learn more about business applications from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") by 2021 [[24]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b24). Finally, MarketsandMarkets research predicted rapid growth for the Digital Twin technology within the next few years, thanks to rising interest in the manufacturing industry to reduce cost and improve [supply chain operations](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/supply-chain-operation "Learn more about supply chain operations from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). As a result, the market for Digital Twin technology was valued at $6.9 billion in 2022. However, it is expected to reach $73.5 billion by 2027- a [CAGR](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/compound-annual-growth-rate "Learn more about CAGR from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of more than 60 percent [[25]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b25).

3. Digital twin technologies
----------------------------

The three main aspects of Digital Twins are data acquisition, [data modeling](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/data-modeling "Learn more about data modeling from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and data application [[26]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b26). Digital Twin uses four technologies to collect and store real-time data, obtain information to provide valuable insights, and create a digital representation of a [physical object](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/physical-object "Learn more about physical object from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). These technologies include the Internet of Things (IoT), [Artificial Intelligence](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/artificial-intelligence "Learn more about Artificial Intelligence from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (AI), [Extended Reality](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/extended-reality "Learn more about Extended Reality from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (XR), and Cloud ([Fig.2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#fig2)). In addition, Digital Twin uses a particular technology, depending on the application type, to a greater or lesser extent.

*   1._Internet of Things (IoT):_ IoT refers to a giant network of connected “things”. The connection is between things–things, people–things, or people–people [[27]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b27). Digital Twins use IoT as its primary technology in every application. By 2027, more than 90 percent of all IoT platforms will have Digital Twinning capability [[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b6). IoT uses sensors to collect data from real-world objects. The data transmitted by IoT is used to create a digital duplication of a physical object. The digital version then can then be analyzed, manipulated, and optimized. IoT constantly updates data and helps Digital Twin applications create a real-time virtual representation of a physical object. Therefore, every Digital Twin application uses IoT as a primary technology. 
*   2._[Cloud Computing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/cloud-computing "Learn more about Cloud Computing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_: Cloud computing refers to delivering hosted services over the Internet. The technology efficiently stores and accesses data over the Internet [[27]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b27). Cloud computing provides Digital Twins with data computing technology and cloud data storage technology. Cloud computing allows Digital Twin, with large volumes of data, to store data in the virtual Cloud and easily access the required information from any location. Cloud computing enables Digital Twins to effectively reduce the [computation time](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/computation-time "Learn more about computation time from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of complex systems and overcome the difficulties of storing large amounts of data [[28]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b28). 
*   3.[Artificial Intelligence (AI)](https://softengi.com/expertise/artificial-intelligence/): As a discipline of computer science, AI seeks to mimic the basis of intelligence to create a new intelligent machine capable of responding like human-to-human intelligence. Areas of AI study include Robotics, image recognition, and language recognition. [Neural Networks](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/neural-network "Learn more about Neural Networks from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [Machine Learning](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/machine-learning "Learn more about Machine Learning from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [Deep Learning](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/deep-learning "Learn more about Deep Learning from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and [expert systems](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/expert-system "Learn more about expert systems from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[[29]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b29), AI can assist Digital Twins by providing an advanced analytical tool capable of automatically analyzing obtained data and providing valuable insights, making predictions about outcomes, and giving suggestions as to how to avoid potential problems [[26]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b26). 
*   4.[Extended Reality (XR)](https://softengi.com/expertise/expanded-reality/) is an umbrella term used to describe immersive technologies like Virtual Reality (VR), [Augmented Reality](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/augmented-reality "Learn more about Augmented Reality from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (AR), and Mixed Reality (MR). These technologies can merge the physical and virtual worlds and extend the reality we experience [[30]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b30). XR creates digital representations of objects where digital and real-world objects co-exist and interact in real-time. Digital Twins utilize XR capabilities to digitally model physical objects, allowing users to interact with digital content.

4. Digital twins use cases and applications
-------------------------------------------

Today, engineering and manufacturing predominantly use Digital Twins to provide accurate virtual representations of objects and simulations of operational processes. Digital Twins applications in operations and supply chain management, especially the role of Digital Twins in terms of operations traceability, transport maintenance, remote assistance, asset visualization, and design [customization](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/customisation "Learn more about customization from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), are reviewed in related publications [[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b13), [[31]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b31), [[32]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b32), [[33]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b33), [[34]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b34), [[35]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b35), [[36]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b36), [[37]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b37), [[38]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b38). The technology is poised to deliver upon its many promises in other industries, including automotive, aerospace, construction, agriculture, mining, utilities, retail, healthcare, military, [natural resources](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/natural-resources "Learn more about natural resources from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and public safety sectors ([Fig.3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#fig3)) [[10]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b10), [[12]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b12), [[19]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b19), [[34]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b34), [[38]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b38), [[39]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b39), [[40]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b40), [[41]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b41), [[42]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b42), [[43]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b43), [[44]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b44). The technology has captured the imagination of scholars, managers, and practitioners worldwide, and numerous [business applications](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/business-application "Learn more about business applications from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of this technology are emerging in the literature [[10]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b10), [[45]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b45), [[46]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b46), [[47]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b47), [[48]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b48), [[49]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b49), [[50]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b50).

# 3 ways your bad data can generate inaccurate digital twins

October 25, 2022 by [Alessandro Chimera](https://www.spotfire.com/blog/author/alessandrochimera/)

![](https://www.spotfire.com/blog/wp-content/uploads/migration/TIBCO-Blog-Image-4.png)

All kinds of industries are adopting and benefitting from digital twins. In manufacturing, digital twins are a simple monitoring solution to track production and can replicate the entire physical shop floor. In the transportation industry, digital twins can replicate trains, process real-time GPS positions of their current position, and detect anomalies by measuring several physical parts. In the healthcare industry, digital twins can replicate patients, understand their conditions, and simulate the effect of certain medications. In building management, digital twins can optimize energy allocation and reduce electricity and gas usage.

Digital twins are a great way to try out new production capacities before launch, detect anomalies in every production phase, increase end product quality, and—with the latest artificial intelligence/machine learning (AI/ML) trends—combine advanced data science capabilities with real-time data to determine when maintenance should happen and prescribe actions.

But, what organizations often don’t consider enough is the exponential growth of generated data in terms of volume, variety, and the number of sources—from increasing data sources such as the Internet of Things (IoT) devices and event streaming. According to [Forbes](https://www.forbes.com/sites/gilpress/2021/12/30/54-predictions-about-the-state-of-data-in-2021/?sh=1b02aba1397d), “From 2010 to 2020, the amount of data created, captured, copied, and consumed in the world increased from 1.2 trillion gigabytes to 59 trillion gigabytes, an almost 5,000 percent growth.”

But why is more data an accuracy risk factor for digital twins?

## It’s all about data quality

Unfortunately, with growing data volumes, data quality tends to decrease. Gartner estimates that the impact due to inaccurate, missing, or duplicated data can [cost organizations](https://www.gartner.com/smarterwithgartner/how-to-improve-your-data-quality) an average of $12.9 million annually. Apart from an immediate revenue impact, poor-quality data can increase the complexity of data management and compromise trust when making business decisions.

Digital twins are becoming more pervasive in monitoring, suggesting actions, or simulating unknown future conditions. When digital twin outputs are influenced by inaccurate or missing data, the entire concept of the virtual copy may contribute to inaccurate decision-making:

## Creating a better twin

How can you avoid inaccurate digital twins created with bad data? A central component of modern data architectures is focusing on data quality. Many platforms can increase data quality, creating trust in data and preventing inaccurate data.

In a data architecture, increasing data quality is a five-step, iterative process:

By creating a so-called data quality firewall, you can provide real-time error detection and correction to protect your digital twins from inaccurate data. Data can be cleansed and corrected in real-time to ensure it receives only high-quality data from any type of data source.

With better and trusted data, digital twins are more accurate and reliable. Data quality is a must, especially when AI/ML models are trained and used for integral decision-making. Decisions may impact a patient’s life, a manufacturing process, or train maintenance—so there’s no room to neglect data quality.

## Are you making bad digital twins?

Worried that your data is creating inaccurate digital twins? Spotfire is here to help. [Contact us](https://www.spotfire.com/contact-us) to learn more about how you can streamline digital twins.

Science & Tech Spotlight: Digital Twins—Virtual Models of People and Objects
============================================================================

**GAO-23-106453** Published: Feb 14, 2023. Publicly Released: Feb 14, 2023. 

*   [](https://www.gao.gov/products/gao-23-106453# "Share on Facebook")

Fast Facts
----------

"Digital twins" are virtual representations of physical objects, processes, or systems—like factories, traffic patterns, and even people. A digital twin is used to predict how changes may affect its physical counterpart. For example, a city could use a digital twin to monitor its energy use to plan for extreme weather.

Digital twins have benefits, like making manufacturing more efficient, but they can raise technical, security, and ethical questions. For example, a doctor could use a patient's digital twin to detect health problems, but there may be data privacy issues. We reviewed some of the opportunities and challenges of this technology.

A digital rendering of a city

![Image 4: a digital rendering of a city](https://www.gao.gov/assets/extracts/e284975d3e4f57268e029680b1102e1e/Fast_Facts_v1_106453-AM.png)

Skip to Highlights

Highlights
----------

Why This Matters
----------------

Digital twins are virtual representations that can mimic and predict the performance of their physical counterparts. Many industries use them to reduce costs, improve engineering design and production, and test supply chains. But some applications—like creating a digital twin of a person—raise technical, privacy, security, and ethical challenges.

The Technology
--------------

**What is it?** Digital twins are virtual representations of people or physical objects, processes, or systems, ranging from vehicles to industrial plants to clinical trial patients. These "living" computational models integrate with data from a physical twin, such that any changes made to the physical twin can automatically lead to changes in the digital twin (see fig. 1). Digital twins can be used to remotely maintain or monitor the physical twin, or predict how it will perform.

Digital twins can be applied across many fields. For example, they can help more efficiently design aircraft parts, predict and avoid supply chain disruptions, and improve traffic safety. They could also monitor living systems or predict hypothetical effects on them for applications in health care, agricultural management, and climate forecasting.

![Image 5](https://www.gao.gov/assets/820/817486.png)

Figure 1. Example of the integration of physical and digital twins.

**How does it work?** Digital twins rely on multiple existing technologies, including the Internet of Things, a network of interconnected "smart" devices that exchange information and collect large amounts of data. Sensors built in or placed on the physical twin or around its environment can collect data continuously. These sensors transmit data that update the digital twin through technologies such as network interfaces and cloud platforms, depending on the physical twin and its location. For example, sensors in a factory may transmit data to a digital twin via local wireless networks, but sensors in public spaces may require cellular networks for adequate connectivity.

Digital twins use these data to dynamically change along with the physical object in real time. This allows users to interact with a 3D visualization, monitor changes, and predict the effect of future events. For example, digital twins of smart cities can be used to predict how extreme weather events will affect the power grid, and what cascading effects might follow. Digital twin data can also be analyzed using artificial intelligence and machine learning to test different scenarios or make predictions for the physical twin. For example, machine learning could automatically recognize an abnormal pattern in a patient's digital twin, enabling a quicker diagnosis and improved treatment (see fig. 2).

![Image 6](https://www.gao.gov/assets/820/817487.png)

Figure 2. Examples of how digital twins can be used in health care.

**How mature is it?** Though the term “digital twin” was coined around 2010, NASA engineers originally applied the concept by building physical and computational models of the Apollo 13 mission vehicles on the ground that were adapted to match the vehicles’ changing conditions in space. Today, the use of digital twins is growing, owing to advances in high-performance computing, rapid data analysis, and smart sensors. Applications in industry include improving operations and predicting or mitigating the effects of manufacturing disruptions.

For example, one company builds digital twins of wind turbines to help manufacturers identify potential problems before scaling up production. The U.S. Navy uses digital twins of shipyards to help inform officials of expected project costs. Digital twins are also being used to more efficiently rebuild areas of a Ukrainian city destroyed in the conflict with Russia, enabling officials to forecast reconstruction costs and improve the city’s infrastructure and resources. In the U.S., a digital twin of the Orlando region allows local government, companies, and nonprofits to simulate and visualize the effects of scenarios such as climate change and new transit routes.

Digital twin technology is less mature in the biological sciences, primarily due to the complexities of living systems found in areas such as health care, agricultural management, and the environment. Researchers are studying whether digital twins of people could improve health care by predicting patient response to different therapies in clinical trials or by diagnosing health conditions using pattern recognition through machine learning. Moreover, the Food and Drug Administration’s strategic plan notes the technology’s cost-saving potential in evaluating medical devices prior to patient use. In addition, a European Union (EU)-funded project plans to use digital twins for personalized medicine by simulating the electromagnetic activity and physiology of patients’ brains to help treat diseases such as Alzheimer’s and epilepsy.

Researchers are also exploring applications in agricultural management by using digital twins to study bee colonies to control honey production and examine the biology of fruit to improve transport and storage. The EU and the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration have initiated projects to build digital twins to monitor global environmental conditions.

**What are some concerns?** Cloud computing services, software, and other products to implement digital twins have potential legal, ethical, and technical issues. According to experts, ethical concerns over data ownership and privacy could result in low public trust. This could occur, for example, if a pharmaceutical company sold health-related data from digital twins without consent. Technical issues such as data quality or accuracy could also reduce public confidence when digital twins are used for decision-making. For example, if data used to train machine learning do not accurately reflect the characteristics of a patient, the predictive analysis by the digital twin could be misleading or even discriminatory.

Digital twins require many interrelated systems and networks for large-scale data collection and transmission, which poses interoperability and cybersecurity issues, according to researchers. Cybersecurity risks could include unauthorized access or modifications to the real data, compromising confidentiality and public trust. Additionally, relevant U.S. infrastructure may not be adequate for such high-performance computing. Researchers have highlighted that the high upfront costs for using digital twins may create access and affordability issues for less affluent city governments or patients whose health insurance does not cover its use.

Researchers also emphasize that standards and regulations are not fully developed to address digital twin implementation, especially for more complex applications. Currently, users rely on a patchwork of standards for data management, security, and networking established by organizations such as the National Institute for Standards and Technology and the International Organization for Standardization.

Opportunities
-------------

*   **Manufacturing and engineering efficiency.** Digital twins monitor in real time to help avoid potential problems, optimizing manufacturing and engineering projects and reducing costs.
*   **Improved health care.** Digital twins could help facilitate predictive and personalized medicine to improve patient outcomes and reduce some health care costs.

Challenges
----------

*   **Privacy and ethical issues.** Security, bias, and data ownership issues, especially for digital twins applied in health care, can reduce public trust in the technology.
*   **Technical and infrastructure barriers.** Digital twins are difficult to scale up for more complex systems, and the current U.S. network infrastructure may not be adequate for expanded use.
*   **Economic costs.** Digital twins can be costly to develop, leading to potential inequities in their use.
*   **Standards and regulations.** Existing standards and regulations for digital twin implementation may not be adequate to address their application in complex systems, especially biological ones.

Policy Context and Questions
----------------------------

*   What options exist for dealing with ethical or equity issues that digital twins could raise, especially in areas such as health care?
*   What steps could help mitigate potential security and privacy risks associated with digital twin technology?
*   How could standards and regulations be further developed for digital twin technology, especially when applied to individuals?

For more information, contact Brian Bothwell at (202) 512-6888 or [bothwellb@gao.gov](mailto:bothwellb@gao.gov).

enriched_points.expanded_content: Digital twins rely on accurate, real-time data from various sources. Poor data quality can lead to incorrect insights, affecting decision-making and operational efficiency.
Integrating digital twins with existing legacy systems poses significant challenges, as outdated technology often lacks compatibility, complicating data flow and interoperability.
Digital twins are susceptible to cyber threats due to their reliance on interconnected systems. Ensuring data security and privacy is crucial to maintain stakeholder trust.
The implementation of digital twins requires substantial initial investments in technology, infrastructure, and training, which can deter organizations, especially smaller ones.
